methodology: Rankings reflect group-stage and knockout results through 30 June 2026, weighted by results quality, goal difference and path difficulty. Title win chances are illustrative estimates aligned with Soccer Stats Hub modelling.

rankings.summary: The only side with a perfect group-stage record among the pre-tournament favourites, France arrive at the Round of 32 with Mbappé, Dembélé and Olise in strong form. A meeting with Sweden in New Jersey is a favourable opener before a potential quarter-final with Paraguay. Didier Deschamps's squad still look the team to beat on paper.

The defending champions navigated Group J without defeat and face Cabo Verde in Miami on 3 July, a tie that should allow Lionel Scaloni to rotate before harder tests. Lautaro Martínez and Enzo Fernández remain the spine of a squad that knows how to win tight knockouts, though the expanded bracket demands greater squad depth than in 2022.

Luis de la Fuente's side topped Group H with seven points but head into the Austria tie on 2 July without Nico Williams and Yeremy Pino, both carrying moderate injuries from the Uruguay finale. Lamine Yamal has been managed carefully since April, so Spain's wing threat is thinner than at Euro 2024 despite their midfield control remaining elite.

Harry Kane became England's all-time leading World Cup scorer in the 2-0 win over Panama, and Thomas Tuchel's side finished top of Group L on seven points. Reece James's hamstring remains a concern before the DR Congo tie in Atlanta, but the bracket has steered England away from Spain and Portugal until a potential semi-final.

Dorival Júnior's side needed Gabriel Martinelli's 95th-minute winner to survive Japan in Houston, exposing defensive lapses that Kaishu Sano punished in the first half. Vinícius Júnior has four goals in the tournament and Neymar made a tearful return against Scotland, but Brazil were far from convincing in their first knockout test.

Mauricio Pochettino's co-hosts won Group D with victories over Paraguay and Australia before a stoppage-time defeat to Turkey in a rotated line-up. Christian Pulisic's calf concern eased after he came off the bench against Turkey, and the squad has arrived in the Bay Area for a home Round of 32 tie with Bosnia and Herzegovina that they are expected to win.

Co-hosts Mexico are the only team yet to concede at the tournament, winning all three group games at the Estadio Azteca before hosting Ecuador in the Round of 32. Altitude and crowd support give them a genuine edge, though they have won only one World Cup knockout match in their history and Ecuador already stunned Germany in the group stage.

Portugal were held by DR Congo and drew with Colombia before advancing, leaving Roberto Martínez with questions about midfield balance heading into a Croatia tie in Toronto. Rafael Leão and Cristiano Ronaldo still provide elite attacking options, but just one win from three group games will concern supporters expecting more from this squad.

Walid Regragui's side followed up their 2022 semi-final run with a dramatic penalty shootout win over the Netherlands, Issa Diop's stoppage-time header forcing extra time before Ismael Saibari converted the winner. Yassine Bounou was decisive again, and Morocco now face Canada in Houston with genuine momentum behind them.

Néstor Lorenzo's side advanced as a best third-placed finisher and face Ghana in Kansas City on 3 July. Luis Díaz remains their main outlet, but a mixed group stage including a draw with Portugal means Colombia have not yet shown the consistency that took them to the 2014 quarter-finals.

Belgium finished behind Egypt in Group G on head-to-head and now meet Senegal in Seattle on 1 July. Domenico Tedesco has attacking quality through Kevin De Bruyne and Jeremy Doku, but defensive inconsistency in the group stage leaves them vulnerable against a physical Senegalese side that scored five against Iraq.

Paraguay produced the biggest shock of the tournament so far, eliminating Germany on penalties in Boston after absorbing 75% possession and 21 shots. Jose Canale held his nerve from the spot, but a probable quarter-final with France in Philadelphia is a far sterner test for a side that averaged under 0.8 goals per game in qualifying.

Sebastián Beccacece's side stunned Germany 2-1 in New Jersey to reach the knockouts as a best third-placed team, with Gonzalo Plata's volley sealing a famous comeback. Travelling to the Estadio Azteca to face Mexico is a daunting assignment, but Ecuador have already shown they can beat elite European opposition.

Switzerland topped Group B ahead of Bosnia and Herzegovina and face Algeria in Vancouver on 2 July. Murat Yakin has a well-drilled defensive unit, but they lacked cutting edge in the group stage and must improve in front of goal to threaten a deep run in the expanded format.

Co-hosts Canada became the first team through to the Round of 16 when Stephen Eustaquio scored in stoppage time against South Africa at SoFi Stadium. Jesse Marsch's side have home support and growing belief, though a last-16 meeting with Morocco in Houston will test whether their progress is sustainable.

Croatia edged Ghana 2-1 to secure second place behind England and now face Portugal in Toronto. Zlatko Dalić still has Luka Modrić pulling the strings, but an ageing squad showed signs of fatigue in the group stage and Portugal's attacking talent makes them underdogs in the Round of 32.

Ralf Rangnick's Austria advanced as a best third-placed side and face Spain in Los Angeles on 2 July, a brutal draw for a team that lacks the individual quality to trouble the European champions for 90 minutes. Their high press caused problems in the group stage, but Spain's midfield control could blunt it.

Senegal reached the knockouts as a best third-placed finisher after a 5-0 demolition of ten-man Iraq, with Sadio Mané leading the line. A Seattle date with Belgium on 1 July is winnable on paper, but they must prove they can beat European opposition after finishing behind France in the group.

The Socceroos finished second behind the USA in Group D and face Egypt in Dallas on 3 July. Graham Arnold has a disciplined, hard-working side, but Mohamed Salah gives Egypt a decisive edge in attack if the tie becomes open.

Algeria held Argentina to a draw in the group stage and advanced in second place, but a Vancouver knockout tie with Switzerland is a step up in quality. Vahid Halilhodžić has defensive organisation, though they scored sparingly across the opening three games.

Erling Haaland has found the net but Norway were inconsistent in the group stage, including a 5-1 loss to France. Ståle Solbakken's side face Ivory Coast in Dallas on 30 June, and even victory would likely mean a Round of 16 meeting with Brazil in New Jersey.

DR Congo held Portugal and beat Uzbekistan to reach their first World Cup knockout stage, with Yoane Wissa and Chancel Mbemba anchoring a compact side under Sébastien Desabre. England in Atlanta on 1 July is a steep step up, though their defensive discipline could keep the score tight.

Bosnia reached the knockouts for the first time after beating Qatar 3-1, with Edin Džeko still the focal point at 40. A Round of 32 tie with co-hosts USA at Levi's Stadium is a mismatch on paper, though Sergej Barbarez will urge his side to make the most of a historic occasion.

The Elephants finished behind Germany in Group E on goal difference and face Norway in Dallas on 30 June. Franck Kessié and Sébastien Haller provide experience, but a leaky defence that conceded against Curaçao and Ecuador leaves them as underdogs against Haaland's Norway.

Sweden advanced as a best third-placed side despite conceding seven goals across three group games, including a 5-1 loss to the Netherlands. Graham Potter must find defensive solidity quickly before facing France at MetLife Stadium on 30 June, where an upset would require something extraordinary.

Egypt topped Group G ahead of Belgium without losing, with Mohamed Salah decisive in attack. Their reward is a Dallas tie with Australia on 3 July, which is winnable, but the path beyond that runs through stronger European and South American sides.

Cabo Verde became one of the feel-good stories of the group stage by advancing as a best third-placed team. A Miami date with Argentina on 3 July is the toughest possible draw, and their limited squad depth makes a prolonged run unlikely despite their organised defending.

Ghana held England to a goalless draw in Boston but lost to Croatia and advanced only as a best third-placed finisher. Otto Addo's side face Colombia in Kansas City on 3 July and will need their counter-attacking best to trouble Luis Díaz and company.
